summarization.a: BF-TEM cross-section of pristine SiO₂ fin structures coated with a ~3.3 nm TiN layer, showing high conformality.
summarization.b: BF-TEM cross-section after partial etching (~0.8 nm) of the TiN layer using NbF₅–CCl₄ at 460 °C, showing minimal surface defects.
summarization.c: BF-TEM cross-section after complete removal of the TiN film, showing intact SiO₂ fins and confirming etch selectivity.

a.question: Does the NbF₅–CCl₄ etch process remove TiN conformally at 460 °C?
Is the surface defect density higher after partial etching compared to pristine TiN?
a.answer: No significant increase in surface defects was observed after ~0.8 nm TiN removal.

a.question: Can this etching method be applied to achieve selective removal of TiN without damaging SiO₂ fins?
a.answer:
Selective removal of TiN
Preservation of SiO₂ fins
High conformality maintained
Potential for advanced 3D nanostructure processing

a.question: Does the conformality of TiN films influence the effectiveness of gas‑phase etching?
a.answer: The conformality of TiN films plays a critical role in the effectiveness of gas‑phase etching. Because the TiN layer uniformly coats even non‑line‑of‑sight features, the NbF₅–CCl₄ etch process can remove material consistently across complex geometries. This structural property enhances isotropic etching and ensures selectivity, making conformal TiN deposition essential for reliable thinning and removal.
